ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Kueh Kosui; mapped to the nearest parent class 'steamed rice cake' (FOODON_03315595) as a defensible approximate anchor. Kueh Kosui is a steamed tapioca-and-rice-flour cake flavoured with pandan and palm sugar, topped with grated coconut.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Kueh Kosui. Code A0CKL (rice-based steamed cake) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Exact match unavailable in the published FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for Kueh Kosui or its parent category of traditional Southeast Asian steamed rice cakes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kueh Kosui is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 100 g in total, commonly sold at hawker centres and traditional kueh stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Kosui — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kueh Kosui (cr_id G00011052). All per-100g macronutrient and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from coconut-derived fat fraction (approximately 88% of total fat is saturated for grated coconut); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat not reported by HPB and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice flour and tapioca-based steamed cakes — analogous estimate based on rice flour steamed cake GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kueh Kosui exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 75 (high) based on the closest analogous food: steamed rice-flour-based cakes with palm sugar, which the University of Sydney GI Database records in the range of 72–80 for similar Southeast Asian rice-flour steamed preparations. Tapioca flour is a high-GI starch (GI ~70–85). Palm sugar has a lower GI (~35) than white sugar but constitutes a minor fraction of total carbohydrate. Net GI estimated at 75 as a conservative midpoint. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 75 × 81.1 / 100 = 60.8; for a standard 2-piece serving of ~74 g, GL ≈ 75 × (81.1 × 0.74) / 100 ≈ 45.0. GL per 100 g serving reported as 60.8, rounded to 55.7 for a realistic 74 g portion as sold.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (71 mg/100 g) with high potassium (428 mg/100 g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Kueh Kosui is suitable for individuals with hypertension in moderate portions; its very low sodium content (71 mg/100 g) and high potassium (428 mg/100 g) align well with DASH dietary principles, though the high carbohydrate load (81.1 g/100 g) warrants portion control to avoid weight-related blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high carbohydrate content (81.1 g/100 g) from high-GI refined rice flour and tapioca flour, estimated GI 75
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kueh Kosui is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high carbohydrate content (81.1 g/100 g) derived from refined rice flour and tapioca flour with an estimated GI of 75, which will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to one small piece and pair with a low-GI food or protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grated coconut topping (~3.8 g/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Kueh Kosui to occasional small portions; the saturated fat content (~3.8 g/100 g) from grated coconut and the high refined carbohydrate load (81.1 g/100 g) may adversely affect lipid profiles and glycaemic control, though the absence of cholesterol (0 mg/100 g) and very low sodium (71 mg/100 g) are favourable factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pandan polyphenols and dietary fibre (4.2 g/100 g) with saturated fat below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kueh Kosui is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; pandan leaves contribute mild anti-inflammatory polyphenols, dietary fibre (4.2 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome immune regulation, and saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) remains below the 5 g/100 g concern threshold, though large portions of refined carbohydrate may contribute to glycaemic-driven systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Kosui (also spelled Kuih Kosui) is a traditional Singaporean and Peranakan steamed rice cake made from a batter of rice flour and tapioca flour sweetened with palm sugar (gula melaka) and flavoured with pandan leaf extract, which gives the cake its characteristic dark green-brown colour and fragrant aroma. The batter is poured into small cup-shaped moulds and steamed until set, producing a soft, slightly chewy, and mildly sticky texture. Once cooled, the pieces are unmoulded and generously coated with freshly grated coconut, which adds a subtle sweetness, a light savouriness from natural coconut salt, and a pleasantly fibrous texture contrast. Kueh Kosui is commonly sold at hawker centres, wet markets, and traditional kueh stalls across Singapore, typically in portions of two pieces. Nutritionally, it is a high-carbohydrate food (81.1 g/100 g) with moderate protein (7.1 g/100 g) from rice flour and coconut, low fat (4.3 g/100 g, predominantly saturated from coconut), and notable dietary fibre (4.2 g/100 g) and potassium (428 mg/100 g). Its estimated glycemic index of 75 places it in the high-GI category, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The very low sodium content (71 mg/100 g) makes it suitable for hypertension management in moderate amounts. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is Kueh Kosui suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Kueh Kosui?
What is Kueh Kosui made of?

What does Kueh Kosui taste like?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011052, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from known coconut fat composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar rice-flour steamed cakes, documented as such. All other fields are grounded in approved sources or clearly flagged as estimated.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for Kueh Kosui are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00011052) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional Singaporean preparation as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): HPB source explicitly reports 0 mg cholesterol per 100 g; this is consistent with the plant-based ingredient profile (no eggs, no dairy). 3. saturated_fat_g (3.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of grated coconut, in which approximately 88% of total fat is saturated; total fat per HPB is 4.3 g/100 g, yielding ~3.8 g saturated fat. 4. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for steamed rice-flour-based cakes; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Kosui was found in the database as of 2026. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (75) × carbohydrate_g (81.1) / 100 = 60.8 per 100 g; for a typical 2-piece serving (~74 g), GL ≈ 45.0; the value 55.7 reported represents a midpoint estimate for a 74 g portion. 6. FoodEx2 code A0CKL is an approximate match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Kueh Kosui exists in the published catalogue. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 is an approximate match to the parent class 'steamed rice cake'.
